county_metadata.record_losses: 1876 courthouse fire destroyed most early records
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Greene County formed 1811 from Amite, Franklin, and Wayne counties. County seat is Leakesville. Burned county: 1876 courthouse fire destroyed most early records. Located in southeast Mississippi in the Piney Woods. Named for General Nathanael Greene.
county_metadata.research_tips: Greene County (formed 1811 from Amite, Franklin, and Wayne) is a burned county due to the 1876 courthouse fire that destroyed most early records. County seat is Leakesville. The Chancery Clerk holds surviving and reconstructed post-fire records. For the burned period (1811-1876), researchers should use BLM GLO federal land patents (glorecords.blm.gov), parent county records (Wayne County especially), and MDAH microfilm of any surviving fragments. Greene County is in the Piney Woods and was historically a timber and subsistence farming area. Freedmen's Bureau records cover the county. Census records from 1820 forward are available through NARA and FamilySearch. Church records from Baptist congregations supplement the burned civil records. Tax rolls at MDAH partially fill gaps.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of Greene County deed books from 1811; Chancery Clerk in Leakesville maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov

source_profiles.survival_notes: Significant losses from 1876 courthouse fire destroyed most early records; reconstructed records and surviving fragments at Chancery Clerk and MDAH

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Grenada County formed 1870 from Carroll, Choctaw, Tallahatchie, and Yalobusha counties. County seat is Grenada. Located in north-central Mississippi at the edge of the Delta. Named for the city of Granada, Spain.
county_metadata.research_tips: Grenada County (formed 1870 from Carroll, Choctaw, Tallahatchie, and Yalobusha) has Grenada as county seat. The Chancery Clerk holds deed books and chancery court records from 1870. For pre-1870 records, check the four parent counties, especially Carroll (Carrollton) and Yalobusha (Water Valley). MDAH has microfilm of Grenada County land and chancery records. Grenada was a Confederate staging area during the Civil War, and military records from the Grenada area are at NARA. BLM GLO land patents document Choctaw Cession entries. Freedmen's Bureau records for the Grenada sub-district document African American families post-war. FamilySearch has digitized Grenada County clerk records. Baptist and Methodist church records from the 1840s-1870s document families before the county's formation.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of Grenada County deed books from 1870; Chancery Clerk in Grenada maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.record_losses: Multiple hurricanes caused record damage; some early records lost to 1969 Hurricane Camille and 2005 Hurricane Katrina
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Hancock County formed 1812 from the Mobile District (formerly Spanish colonial territory). County seat is Bay St. Louis. Gulf Coast county with French and Spanish colonial heritage. Multiple hurricane damage events including Camille (1969) and Katrina (2005) have affected records.
county_metadata.research_tips: Hancock County (formed 1812 from Mobile District, formerly Spanish colonial territory) has Bay St. Louis as county seat. Hancock County has experienced repeated hurricane damage to records, most severely from Hurricane Camille (1969) and Hurricane Katrina (2005), making it a burned-equivalent county. The Chancery Clerk holds surviving records. MDAH has microfilm of some early Hancock County records made before the storms. Hancock County has a unique French and Spanish colonial heritage -- records of the Biloxi-Bay St. Louis coast are at MDAH and the Mississippi Provincial Archives. Catholic church records (Our Lady of the Gulf, Bay St. Louis, and others) predate Protestant records on the coast. BLM GLO land patents document early federal land entries. The Hancock County Historical Society has worked to preserve and reconstruct records after Katrina. FamilySearch has some pre-storm microfilm.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of Hancock County deed books from 1812; Chancery Clerk in Bay St. Louis maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.record_losses: Hurricane Katrina (2005) caused extensive damage to courthouse records; some earlier hurricane damage as well
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Harrison County formed 1841 from Hancock and Jackson counties. County seat is Gulfport, with Biloxi as the other major city. Gulf Coast county. Hurricane Katrina (2005) caused extensive damage to courthouse records. Biloxi was the first capital of French Louisiana (1720). Keesler Air Force Base is in Biloxi.
county_metadata.research_tips: Harrison County (formed 1841 from Hancock and Jackson) has Gulfport as county seat (Biloxi is the other major city). Hurricane Katrina (2005) caused extensive damage to courthouse records, making this a burned-equivalent county. The Chancery Clerk in Gulfport holds surviving records. MDAH has microfilm of many Harrison County records made before the storms. Harrison County has strong French colonial heritage -- Biloxi was the first capital of French Louisiana (1720). Catholic church records from Nativity BVM in Biloxi (1843) are important. MDAH holds the J.L. Power papers and other Gulf Coast collections. Keesler Air Force Base (est. 1941) records and military personnel files at NARA are relevant for 20th-century families. The seafood industry and railroad brought diverse immigrant communities. FamilySearch has digitized available Harrison County records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of Harrison County deed books from 1841; Chancery Clerk in Gulfport maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Hinds County formed 1821 from the Choctaw Cession of 1820. County seat is Jackson, the state capital and largest city. Named for General Thomas Hinds. MDAH, the state vital records office, and other state agencies are headquartered in Jackson.
county_metadata.research_tips: Hinds County (formed 1821 from the Choctaw Cession of 1820) contains Jackson, the state capital and largest city. The Chancery Clerk holds deed books from 1821 and extensive chancery court records. MDAH is physically located in Jackson and holds the most comprehensive collection of Mississippi records. The Eudora Welty Library and the Jackson-Hinds Library System have local history collections. Hinds County was a major slaveholding area, and MDAH holds plantation records, slave schedules, and estate inventories for the county. Freedmen's Bureau headquarters for Mississippi was in Jackson -- records at NARA (M826, M1907) are extensive. BLM GLO land patents document Choctaw Cession entries. City directories for Jackson from the 1860s onward are at MDAH. The state vital records office (Mississippi State Department of Health) is in Jackson. FamilySearch has extensively digitized Hinds County records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of Hinds County deed books from 1821; Chancery Clerk in Jackson maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov

source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete from 1821; original deed books held by Chancery Clerk in False

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Holmes County formed 1833 from the Choctaw Cession of 1830. County seat is Lexington. Located at the eastern edge of the Mississippi Delta. Significant in the civil rights movement of the 1960s. Named for David Holmes, first governor of Mississippi.
county_metadata.research_tips: Holmes County (formed 1833 from Choctaw Cession) has Lexington as county seat. The Chancery Clerk holds deed books from 1833 and chancery court records. MDAH has microfilm of Holmes County land records, chancery minutes, and marriage books. Holmes County was a major cotton-producing county in the Delta region with a large enslaved population. Estate inventories and slave schedules at MDAH are essential for African American research. Freedmen's Bureau records for central Mississippi cover Holmes County. BLM GLO land patents document Choctaw Cession entries. Holmes County was also significant in the civil rights movement -- the Holmes County civil rights collection is at MDAH and the Tougaloo College archives. FamilySearch has digitized Holmes County clerk records. Baptist and Methodist church records from the 1830s document early families.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of Holmes County deed books from 1833; Chancery Clerk in Lexington maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Humphreys County formed 1918 from Holmes, Sunflower, Washington, and Yazoo counties. County seat is Belzoni, the 'Catfish Capital of the World.' A Delta county heavily impacted by the 1927 Great Flood. Named for Benjamin G. Humphreys, Civil War governor.
county_metadata.research_tips: Humphreys County (formed 1918 from Holmes, Sunflower, Washington, and Yazoo) has Belzoni as county seat. The Chancery Clerk holds deed books and chancery court records from 1918. For pre-1918 records, check the four parent counties, especially Holmes (Lexington) and Yazoo (Yazoo City). Humphreys County is a Delta county with an overwhelmingly African American population through much of its history. MDAH has microfilm of available records. Plantation records and tenant farming documentation at MDAH are important for 20th-century research. The 1927 Great Flood heavily impacted Humphreys County -- Red Cross relief records at NARA document displaced families. FamilySearch has some Humphreys County records. Tax rolls at MDAH help trace landholding patterns.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of Humphreys County deed books from 1918; Chancery Clerk in Belzoni maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.record_losses: 1872 courthouse fire and 1877 flood damaged early records; some deed books lost
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Issaquena County formed 1844 from Washington County. County seat is Mayersville. Burned county: 1872 courthouse fire and 1877 flood damaged early records. Mississippi's least populous county. Delta floodplain county. The name is Choctaw for 'deer's head.'
county_metadata.research_tips: Issaquena County (formed 1844 from Washington) is a burned county due to the 1872 courthouse fire and 1877 flood that damaged early records. County seat is Mayersville. Issaquena is the least populous county in Mississippi. The Chancery Clerk holds surviving and reconstructed records. For the burned period, use BLM GLO federal land patents (glorecords.blm.gov), Washington County (Greenville) records for pre-1844 entries, and MDAH microfilm. Issaquena County is in the Delta floodplain and was dominated by large cotton plantations with an overwhelmingly enslaved population. Freedmen's Bureau records at NARA document post-war African American communities. The 1927 Great Flood devastated the county. Census records from 1850 forward are available. FamilySearch has limited Issaquena County records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of Issaquena County deed books from 1844; Chancery Clerk in Mayersville maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov

source_profiles.repository: Issaquena County Chancery Clerk, True / Mississippi Department of Archives and History, Jackson
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Mississippi uses Chancery Court for probate (unique system -- no separate probate court). Wills, estates, guardianships, and land partitions for Issaquena County from 1844; MDAH has microfilm of chancery minutes and estate files
source_profiles.survival_notes: Significant losses due to 1872 courthouse fire and 1877 flood damaged early records

source_profiles.repository: Issaquena County Chancery Clerk, True / FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage records for Issaquena County from 1844; FamilySearch has indexed many Mississippi county marriage records; state marriage registration began 1926 through Mississippi State Department of Health

source_profiles.source_name: Issaquena County Federal Census Records
source_profiles.repository: NARA / FamilySearch / Ancestry
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census for Issaquena County from 1850 through 1950; includes slave schedules 1850-1860 listing enslaved persons by owner; agricultural and manufacturing schedules provide economic data; the 1890 census was destroyed nationally

source_profiles.source_name: Issaquena County Enslaved Persons and Plantation Records -- MDAH / NARA
source_profiles.repository: Mississippi Department of Archives and History, Jackson / NARA
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Issaquena County had a very large enslaved population. MDAH holds plantation records, estate inventories listing enslaved persons by name, slave schedules (1850, 1860), and slaveholder correspondence. Freedmen's Bureau records at NARA (M826, M1907) document formerly enslaved families post-war

source_profiles.source_name: Issaquena County Tax Rolls -- MDAH
source_profiles.repository: Mississippi Department of Archives and History, Jackson
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Tax rolls for Issaquena County listing property owners, acreage, livestock, and enslaved persons (pre-1865); essential for tracking family landholding and wealth; MDAH holds microfilm of county tax rolls
source_profiles.survival_notes: Some losses due to courthouse destruction

source_profiles.source_name: Issaquena County Cemetery Records -- Find A Grave / MSGenWeb
source_profiles.repository: Find A Grave / MSGenWeb / Local historical societies
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Cemetery transcriptions for Issaquena County churches, community cemeteries, and family burial grounds; Find A Grave has growing coverage; MSGenWeb volunteers have transcribed many Issaquena County cemeteries; particularly valuable when civil records are incomplete or destroyed

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Itawamba County formed 1836 from the Chickasaw Cession of 1832. County seat is Fulton. Located in northeast Mississippi. Named for Chickasaw chief Levi Colbert's Chickasaw name Itawamba. Small-farm rather than plantation economy.
county_metadata.research_tips: Itawamba County (formed 1836 from the Chickasaw Cession of 1832) has Fulton as county seat. The Chancery Clerk holds deed books from 1836 and chancery court records. Itawamba County is in northeast Mississippi and was historically a small-farm rather than plantation area. MDAH has microfilm of Itawamba County land records, marriage books, and chancery minutes. BLM GLO land patents (glorecords.blm.gov) document Chickasaw Cession entries. The county had a smaller enslaved population than Delta counties. Church records from Baptist and Methodist congregations are especially important for Itawamba County families. FamilySearch has digitized Itawamba County clerk records. Tax rolls and personal property records at MDAH trace early settlers. Many families migrated from Tennessee, Alabama, and the Carolinas.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of Itawamba County deed books from 1836; Chancery Clerk in Fulton maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov

source_profiles.repository: NARA (Record Group 75) / Bureau of Land Management, General Land Office
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Itawamba County was formed from Chickasaw Cession lands (Treaty of Pontotoc, 1832). BLM GLO records document federal land sales following removal. NARA Record Group 75 holds Chickasaw removal rolls, allotment records, and agency correspondence

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Civil War Confederate service records for Itawamba County soldiers; Mississippi contributed heavily to the Confederate army; MDAH holds state adjutant general records; NARA has compiled Confederate service records; Fold3 has digitized many; also includes WWI/WWII draft registrations

county_metadata.record_losses: Hurricane damage across multiple storms; some early records lost
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Jackson County formed 1812 from the Mobile District (formerly Spanish colonial territory). County seat is Pascagoula. Gulf Coast county with French and Spanish colonial heritage. Hurricane damage across multiple storms has affected records. Ingalls Shipbuilding (now Huntington Ingalls) is a major employer.
county_metadata.research_tips: Jackson County (formed 1812 from Mobile District, formerly Spanish colonial territory) has Pascagoula as county seat. Like other Gulf Coast counties, Jackson County has suffered hurricane damage to records across multiple storms. The Chancery Clerk holds surviving records. MDAH has microfilm of early Jackson County records. The county has French and Spanish colonial heritage -- Pascagoula was part of French Louisiana and later Spanish West Florida. Catholic church records from the coast predate civil records. The Jackson County Genealogical Society has worked to index and preserve local records. Ingalls Shipbuilding (now Huntington Ingalls) employment records may be useful for 20th-century research. BLM GLO land patents document early federal entries. FamilySearch has digitized available Jackson County records. The Pascagoula River watershed and barrier islands influenced settlement.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of Jackson County deed books from 1812; Chancery Clerk in Pascagoula maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov
